Tomato - Early Blight.description: Early blight is a common fungal disease caused by Alternaria solani that affects tomato plants. It typically appears as dark brown to black concentric rings on older leaves.
Tomato - Early Blight.cause: Caused by the fungus Alternaria solani. Spreads through wind, rain splash, and contaminated tools. Thrives in warm (24-29°C), humid conditions.
Tomato - Early Blight.symptoms: Dark brown spots with concentric rings (target-like pattern) on lower/older leaves. Yellowing around spots. Leaves may drop prematurely. Stem lesions and fruit rot in severe cases.
Tomato - Early Blight.organic_treatment: Remove and destroy infected leaves immediately. Apply neem oil spray (2-3ml/litre). Use Trichoderma viride as a biocontrol agent. Apply compost tea as a foliar spray.
Tomato - Early Blight.chemical_treatment: Apply Mancozeb (2.5g/litre) or Chlorothalonil fungicide. Spray Copper oxychloride (3g/litre) at 10-day intervals. Use systemic fungicide like Azoxystrobin for severe infections.
Tomato - Early Blight.prevention: Practice crop rotation (3-year cycle). Ensure adequate plant spacing for air circulation. Avoid overhead watering. Mulch around plants to prevent soil splash. Remove plant debris after harvest.

Tomato - Late Blight.description: Late blight is a devastating disease caused by the oomycete Phytophthora infestans. It can destroy an entire crop within days under favourable conditions.
Tomato - Late Blight.cause: Caused by Phytophthora infestans. Spreads rapidly in cool (10-25°C), wet conditions. Can be carried by wind over long distances.
Tomato - Late Blight.symptoms: Large, irregular, water-soaked lesions on leaves that turn brown/black. White fuzzy growth on underside of leaves in humid conditions. Firm, dark lesions on stems. Brown, firm rot on fruits.
Tomato - Late Blight.organic_treatment: Remove all infected plant parts immediately and burn them. Apply copper-based organic fungicide (Bordeaux mixture). Spray baking soda solution (1 tablespoon per gallon of water).
Tomato - Late Blight.chemical_treatment: Apply Metalaxyl + Mancozeb (2.5g/litre) immediately upon detection. Use Cymoxanil-based fungicides. Spray Chlorothalonil as a preventive measure during wet periods.
Tomato - Late Blight.prevention: Plant resistant varieties. Avoid planting near potato fields. Ensure good drainage and air circulation. Water at the base of plants, never on foliage. Destroy volunteer plants and crop debris.

Tomato - Leaf Mold.description: Leaf mold is a fungal disease caused by Passalora fulva (formerly Cladosporium fulvum), mainly affecting greenhouse-grown tomatoes.
Tomato - Leaf Mold.cause: Caused by the fungus Passalora fulva. Thrives in high humidity (>85%) and moderate temperatures (22-24°C). Spread by airborne spores.
Tomato - Leaf Mold.symptoms: Pale green to yellowish spots on upper leaf surface. Olive-green to brown velvety mold on lower leaf surface. Leaves curl and drop in severe infections.
Tomato - Leaf Mold.organic_treatment: Improve ventilation and reduce humidity. Apply neem oil or sulfur-based organic fungicide. Remove and destroy severely affected leaves.
Tomato - Leaf Mold.chemical_treatment: Apply Chlorothalonil or Mancozeb-based fungicide. Use systemic fungicides like Difenoconazole for severe cases.
Tomato - Leaf Mold.prevention: Maintain good ventilation in greenhouses. Keep humidity below 85%. Avoid overhead irrigation. Use resistant tomato varieties. Space plants adequately.

Tomato - Septoria Leaf Spot.description: Septoria leaf spot is caused by the fungus Septoria lycopersici. It is one of the most common foliar diseases of tomato.
Tomato - Septoria Leaf Spot.cause: Caused by the fungus Septoria lycopersici. Spreads through rain splash and contaminated tools. Overwinters in plant debris.
Tomato - Septoria Leaf Spot.symptoms: Small, circular spots (1-3mm) with dark borders and grey/tan centres. Tiny black dots (pycnidia) visible in spot centres. Lower leaves affected first. Heavy defoliation in severe cases.
Tomato - Septoria Leaf Spot.organic_treatment: Remove infected leaves promptly. Apply copper-based fungicide. Spray with baking soda solution. Mulch to prevent soil splash onto leaves.
Tomato - Septoria Leaf Spot.chemical_treatment: Apply Chlorothalonil or Mancozeb at 7-10 day intervals. Use Azoxystrobin for systemic protection.
Tomato - Septoria Leaf Spot.prevention: Practice crop rotation. Remove crop debris. Avoid overhead watering. Stake and prune plants for air circulation. Water in the morning so leaves dry quickly.

Tomato - Healthy.description: The plant appears healthy with no signs of disease.
Tomato - Healthy.symptoms: No symptoms. Leaves are green, firm, and showing normal growth patterns.
Tomato - Healthy.organic_treatment: No treatment needed. Continue good practices: compost tea, neem oil as preventive spray, companion planting.
Tomato - Healthy.chemical_treatment: No chemical treatment required. Consider preventive fungicide applications during rainy season.
Tomato - Healthy.prevention: Maintain proper watering schedule. Ensure adequate nutrition. Practice crop rotation. Monitor regularly for early signs of stress.

Potato - Early Blight.description: Early blight in potatoes is caused by Alternaria solani, the same pathogen that affects tomatoes. It primarily damages foliage and can reduce tuber yield.
Potato - Early Blight.cause: Caused by Alternaria solani. Favoured by warm temperatures (24-29°C) and alternating wet/dry periods. Overwinters in crop debris and soil.
Potato - Early Blight.symptoms: Dark brown spots with concentric rings on older leaves. Collar rot at soil level. Dark, sunken lesions on tubers with raised borders.
Potato - Early Blight.organic_treatment: Remove infected foliage. Apply Trichoderma harzianum as biological control. Spray neem oil (2ml/litre). Use Bordeaux mixture.
Potato - Early Blight.chemical_treatment: Apply Mancozeb 75% WP (2.5g/litre). Use Propineb or Chlorothalonil as preventive sprays. Apply Azoxystrobin for curative treatment.
Potato - Early Blight.prevention: Use certified disease-free seed potatoes. Hill plants adequately. Harvest at maturity and cure tubers before storage. Maintain 3-year crop rotation.

Potato - Late Blight.description: Potato late blight, caused by Phytophthora infestans, is historically one of the most destructive crop diseases. It caused the Great Irish Famine.
Potato - Late Blight.cause: Caused by Phytophthora infestans. Requires cool (10-20°C), wet conditions. Can spread extremely rapidly through wind-dispersed sporangia.
Potato - Late Blight.symptoms: Water-soaked dark spots on leaves that enlarge rapidly. White mold on leaf undersides in humid conditions. Dark brown to purple lesions on stems. Reddish-brown rot in tubers.
Potato - Late Blight.organic_treatment: Remove and destroy all infected plant parts immediately. Apply copper hydroxide spray. Use Bordeaux mixture (1%). Harvest early to prevent tuber infection.
Potato - Late Blight.chemical_treatment: Apply Metalaxyl-M + Mancozeb immediately. Use Cymoxanil + Mancozeb for curative treatment. Apply Dimethomorph as preventive during high-risk periods.
Potato - Late Blight.prevention: Plant resistant varieties. Use certified seed potatoes. Avoid overhead irrigation. Destroy volunteer plants. Monitor weather forecasts for blight-favourable conditions.

Corn - Common Rust.description: Common rust is caused by the fungus Puccinia sorghi and is widespread in maize/corn growing regions. It is usually more of an aesthetic issue but can reduce yields in severe cases.
Corn - Common Rust.cause: Caused by the fungus Puccinia sorghi. Spores are wind-dispersed over long distances. Favoured by moderate temperatures (16-25°C) and high humidity.
Corn - Common Rust.symptoms: Small, elongated, brick-red to brown pustules (uredinia) on both leaf surfaces. Pustules may darken to black as the plant matures. Severe infections cause leaf chlorosis and drying.
Corn - Common Rust.organic_treatment: Remove severely infected leaves. Maintain plant vigour with balanced fertilization. Spray sulfur-based fungicide as a preventive measure.
Corn - Common Rust.chemical_treatment: Apply Propiconazole or Tebuconazole fungicide. Use Azoxystrobin + Propiconazole for severe infections. Spray Mancozeb as a preventive during early season.
Corn - Common Rust.prevention: Plant resistant hybrids. Early planting to avoid peak rust periods. Maintain balanced nutrition. Scout fields regularly during warm, humid weather.

Corn - Northern Leaf Blight.description: Northern leaf blight (NLB) is caused by the fungus Exserohilum turcicum (formerly Helminthosporium turcicum). It can cause significant yield loss in susceptible varieties.
Corn - Northern Leaf Blight.cause: Caused by Exserohilum turcicum. Spreads via wind-dispersed conidia. Favoured by moderate temperatures (18-27°C) and wet weather. Overwinters in corn debris.
Corn - Northern Leaf Blight.symptoms: Long, elliptical, greyish-green to tan lesions (2.5-15 cm) on leaves. Lesions may coalesce and blight entire leaves. Lower leaves affected first.
Corn - Northern Leaf Blight.organic_treatment: Remove crop debris after harvest. Practice crop rotation with non-host crops. Apply Trichoderma-based biological fungicides.
Corn - Northern Leaf Blight.chemical_treatment: Apply Propiconazole at early infection stage. Use Azoxystrobin + Propiconazole for broad-spectrum protection.
Corn - Northern Leaf Blight.prevention: Plant resistant hybrids. Rotate crops (avoid corn-on-corn). Till or remove corn residue. Scout regularly from tasseling onwards.

Rice - Brown Spot.description: Brown spot is caused by the fungus Bipolaris oryzae (syn. Cochliobolus miyabeanus). It is associated with nutrient-deficient soils.
Rice - Brown Spot.cause: Caused by Bipolaris oryzae. Spread through infected seeds and wind. Favoured by nutrient-poor soils, especially potassium and silicon deficiency.
Rice - Brown Spot.symptoms: Small, oval to circular brown spots with grey centres on leaves. Spots may coalesce causing leaf blight. Infected grains show brown discoloration.
Rice - Brown Spot.organic_treatment: Treat seeds with Trichoderma viride before sowing. Apply farmyard manure and organic compost. Spray Pseudomonas fluorescens-based biopesticide.
Rice - Brown Spot.chemical_treatment: Apply Mancozeb 75% WP (2.5g/litre). Spray Propiconazole (1ml/litre) for curative treatment. Treat seeds with Thiram before sowing.
Rice - Brown Spot.prevention: Use certified disease-free seeds. Maintain balanced soil nutrition (N, P, K, Si). Avoid water stress. Remove crop debris.

Cotton - Bacterial Blight.description: Bacterial blight of cotton is caused by Xanthomonas citri pv. malvacearum. It affects leaves, stems, and bolls.
Cotton - Bacterial Blight.cause: Caused by Xanthomonas citri pv. malvacearum. Spread through rain splash, wind, and contaminated seeds. Enters through wounds and stomata.
Cotton - Bacterial Blight.symptoms: Angular, water-soaked lesions on leaves that turn brown/black. Lesions follow leaf veins. Black arm symptoms on stems. Boll rot in severe cases.
Cotton - Bacterial Blight.organic_treatment: Use disease-free seeds. Apply copper-based bactericides. Spray Streptocycline (0.01%) + Copper oxychloride.
Cotton - Bacterial Blight.chemical_treatment: Apply Streptomycin sulfate (0.05g/litre). Spray Copper oxychloride (3g/litre) at 10-day intervals. Seed treatment with Carboxin.
Cotton - Bacterial Blight.prevention: Use resistant varieties. Acid-delint seeds for treatment. Practice crop rotation. Remove and destroy infected plant debris. Avoid working in wet fields.
